model_narrative: This is a Resource-Task-Network scheduling problem for a small batch chemical plant. Across a discrete time horizon the plant decides, at each time point, which processing tasks to trigger and how large a batch each triggered task should run. Tracking the running inventory of every resource over time is part of the bookkeeping. The goal is to meet the external material schedule while triggering as few task executions as possible, so the objective minimizes the total number of task triggers over the horizon.

sets.name: I
sets.doc: processing tasks (reactions)

sets.name: R
sets.doc: resources: reactants, products, and the reactor-availability resource V

sets.name: T
sets.doc: time points; point 0 only carries the initial inventory

sets.name: T1
sets.doc: decision time points, excluding the initialization point 0

sets.name: Ir
sets.members: Ir[r] is the set of tasks that touch resource r
sets.doc: for each resource, the tasks connected to it in the network

params.name: mu
params.doc: fixed consumption or production of a resource by a task at offset theta time points after the task is triggered; sign convention: negative is consumption, positive is production; offset 0 is the trigger instant, offset tau is reaction completion

params.name: nu
params.doc: batch-size-proportional consumption or production of a resource by a task at offset theta after trigger; same sign convention as mu; for reactants this is the per-unit draw at trigger, for products the per-unit yield at completion

params.name: tau
params.doc: processing duration of each task in time points

params.name: Vmin
params.doc: minimum batch size when a task is run

params.name: Vmax
params.doc: maximum batch size when a task is run

params.name: X0
params.doc: initial inventory of each resource at time point 0

params.name: Xmin
params.doc: minimum allowed inventory (safety stock) of each resource

params.name: Xmax
params.doc: maximum allowed inventory (storage capacity) of each resource

params.name: Pi
params.doc: scheduled external inflow or outflow of a resource at each time point; positive is delivery in, negative is demand out

vars.name: X
vars.doc: inventory level of each resource at each time point; X[r,0] is fixed to the initial inventory

vars.name: N
vars.doc: 1 if task i is triggered at time point t, else 0

vars.name: E
vars.doc: batch size of task i triggered at time point t; zero if not triggered

objective.expr_var: total number of task triggers over the horizon

constraints.name: X[r,t]
constraints.intent: For every resource at every decision time point, the inventory carried at that point must equal the inventory carried at the previous point, adjusted by the net effect of all tasks acting on that resource and by the scheduled external inflow or outflow for that resource at that point. A task contributes to a resource according to how long ago it was triggered, combining a fixed per-trigger effect with an effect proportional to its batch size, and only contributions from triggers that have already occurred within the task duration count.

constraints.name: X[r,t]
constraints.intent: For every resource at every decision time point, the inventory carried at that point must stay at or above the minimum allowed level for that resource.

constraints.name: X[r,t]
constraints.intent: For every resource at every decision time point, the inventory carried at that point must stay at or below the storage capacity for that resource.

constraints.name: E[i,t]
constraints.intent: For every task at every decision time point, if the task is triggered then its batch size must be at least the minimum batch size for that task, and if it is not triggered its batch size is forced to zero.

constraints.name: E[i,t]
constraints.intent: For every task at every decision time point, the batch size cannot exceed the maximum batch size for that task when it is triggered, and must be zero when the task is not triggered.

query: Operations wants to model a busier run — suppose the schedule must fire at least 4 task triggerings in total. What does that do to the plan?
expected_pyomo: model.min_starts = Constraint(expr=sum(model.N[k] for k in model.N) >= 4)

query: Sales is pitching a bulk commitment: the total batch volume processed across all three reactions, summed over the whole horizon, has to reach at least 190 units. How many task triggerings does the schedule need to deliver that?
expected_pyomo: model.volume_floor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.E[i, t] for i in model.I for t in model.T1) >= 190)

query: There's a two-product supply deal on the table, and both halves land together: at least 100 units of total Rx2 batch volume and at least 100 units of total Rx3 batch volume over the horizon. With both commitments in force, how many triggerings does the plan use?
expected_pyomo:
model.rx2_vol = Constraint(expr=sum(model.E['Rx2', t] for t in model.T1) >= 100)
model.rx3_vol = Constraint(expr=sum(model.E['Rx3', t] for t in model.T1) >= 100)

query: We're evaluating splitting Rx1 into multiple smaller runs — require Rx1 to be triggered at least twice over the horizon. How many total triggerings does the plan then use?
expected_pyomo: model.split_rx1 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.N['Rx1', t] for t in model.T1) >= 2)

query: Each reaction fires exactly once, early, and the late deliveries get served out of stockpile. Shouldn't every task fire at least once somewhere in periods 6 through 10, so the late draw-downs come from fresh runs? What's the cost the model is dodging there?

query: Running Rx2 as one big batch seems risky. Wouldn't it be safer to split it into at least two separate Rx2 firings? Walk me through what that costs in extra triggerings.
expected_pyomo: model.split_rx2 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.N['Rx2', t] for t in model.T1) >= 2)

query: Rx1 feeds our flagship product, yet the plan gives it the same single batch as everything else. Shouldn't Rx1's total batch volume over the horizon run at least one and a half times Rx3's total? What's the model seeing that keeps them on equal footing?
expected_pyomo: model.rx1_dominance = Constraint(expr=sum(model.E['Rx1', t] for t in model.T1) >= 1.5 * sum(model.E['Rx3', t] for t in model.T1))

objective.expr_var: total number of task triggers over the horizon

constraints.name: X[r,t]
constraints.intent: For every resource at every decision time point, the inventory carried at that point must equal the inventory carried at the previous point, adjusted by the net effect of all tasks acting on that resource and by the scheduled external inflow or outflow for that resource at that point. A task contributes to a resource according to how long ago it was triggered, combining a fixed per-trigger effect with an effect proportional to its batch size, and only contributions from triggers that have already occurred within the task duration count.

constraints.name: X[r,t]
constraints.intent: For every resource at every decision time point, the inventory carried at that point must stay at or above the minimum allowed level for that resource.

constraints.name: X[r,t]
constraints.intent: For every resource at every decision time point, the inventory carried at that point must stay at or below the storage capacity for that resource.

constraints.name: E[i,t]
constraints.intent: For every task at every decision time point, if the task is triggered then its batch size must be at least the minimum batch size for that task, and if it is not triggered its batch size is forced to zero.

constraints.name: E[i,t]
constraints.intent: For every task at every decision time point, the batch size cannot exceed the maximum batch size for that task when it is triggered, and must be zero when the task is not triggered.

query: Rx2 goes out as one big batch near the reactor's limit, and a batch that size makes QA nervous. Couldn't we hold every single Rx2 batch to at most 40 units, whichever period it runs in? What does the model say that discipline costs us?
